requirements.english: TOEFL (typ. iBT 100) or IELTS (typ. 7.0), Cambridge English, or Duolingo; waived for native speakers or a recent degree from an English-medium institution in the US/UK/Ireland/Canada/Australia/NZ.
requirements.admission_model_note: Applicants are admitted by a departmental admissions committee via holistic review routed by stated area of interest; students typically identify a research advisor during the first year through lab visits and research assistantships rather than being matched at admission.

funding.note: All PhD students are fully supported for the program's duration with tuition, a living stipend, and health insurance via fellowships and research/teaching assistantships.

requirements.contact_note: Admission is by committee; contacting individual faculty before applying is not required and does not affect the decision.

faculty.name: Pablo Jarillo-Herrero
faculty.title: Cecil and Ida Green Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Jarillo-Herrero pioneered 'twistronics,' discovering superconductivity and correlated insulating states in magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene. Recent work (2024-2025) measured the superfluid stiffness of magic-angle twisted bilayer and trilayer graphene, revealing unconventional pairing, and explored topological superconductivity and quantum-geometric effects in moiré systems. His group also demonstrated on-chip multi-degree-of-freedom control of two-dimensional materials and studied plasmons and correlated phases in engineered van der Waals heterostructures. He was named a 2024 Clarivate Citation Laureate for this work.

faculty.name: Senthil Todadri
faculty.title: William and Emma Rogers Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Senthil develops theories of strongly correlated quantum matter beyond the Landau paradigm, including quantum spin liquids, deconfined quantum criticality, and non-Fermi-liquid metals. His recent work applies these ideas to moiré heterostructures, predicting ferromagnetism and quantum anomalous Hall effects later observed in twisted graphene experiments, and to the correlated and superconducting phases of magic-angle graphene. He continues to study continuous Mott transitions, fractionalized Fermi liquids, and the interplay of topology, gauge structure, and criticality in two-dimensional electron systems.

faculty.name: Wolfgang Ketterle
faculty.title: John D. MacArthur Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Ketterle, a 2001 Nobel laureate for Bose-Einstein condensation, studies ultracold atomic gases, superfluidity, spin physics, and ultracold molecules. Recent work (2024) realized a bilayer system of dipolar atoms separated by roughly 50 nanometers, probing dipolar interactions at unprecedented length scales. His group has investigated coherent and incoherent light scattering by single-atom wavepackets, probed quantum many-body dynamics via subsystem Loschmidt echoes, and developed high-density magneto-optical trapping of calcium monofluoride molecules for studies of ultracold chemistry and quantum simulation.

faculty.name: Nergis Mavalvala
faculty.title: Curtis and Kathleen Marble Professor of Astrophysics; Dean of the School of Science
faculty.summary: Mavalvala works on gravitational-wave detection with LIGO and on quantum measurement science. She and collaborators developed and deployed frequency-dependent squeezed light in LIGO, and in 2024 reported squeezing the detectors' quantum noise below the standard quantum limit, broadening sensitivity across the astrophysical frequency band. Her group also explores macroscopic quantum effects and optomechanical squeezing to prepare and measure mechanical oscillators near their quantum ground state, extending the reach of gravitational-wave observatories. She serves as Dean of MIT's School of Science.

faculty.name: Tracy Slatyer
faculty.title: Professor of Physics; Director, MIT Center for Theoretical Physics
faculty.summary: Slatyer is a theoretical astrophysicist searching for signatures of dark matter in astrophysical and cosmological data. She co-discovered the 'Fermi bubbles,' giant gamma-ray structures above and below the galactic plane. Recent work develops precision predictions for photon signals from annihilating dark matter, models dark-sector forces and their imprint on the early universe and cosmic microwave background, and applies data-analysis and machine-learning methods to gamma-ray observations of the Galactic Center. She directs the MIT Center for Theoretical Physics.

faculty.name: Or Hen
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Hen studies short-range correlations (SRCs) in atomic nuclei: fleeting, high-momentum neutron-proton pairs probed by high-energy electron, photon, proton, and ion scattering. His group has linked SRCs to the nuclear EMC effect, connecting nucleon-structure modifications to two-nucleon dynamics, and extended measurements to neutrino scattering and dense nuclear matter. Recent work (2024-2025) measured SRCs via bremsstrahlung gamma-ray emission in heavy-ion collisions and examined three-body correlations and their consequences for the neutron-star equation of state.

faculty.name: Soonwon Choi
faculty.title: Victor F. Weisskopf Associate Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Choi is a theorist studying far-from-equilibrium dynamics of strongly interacting quantum many-body systems and their applications to quantum information. He predicted and helped observe the emergence of universal random-state ensembles ('deep thermalization') under natural Hamiltonian dynamics, enabling efficient benchmarking of quantum simulators via many-body quantum chaos. His recent work analyzes measurement-induced phase transitions in the dynamics of quantum information and designs protocols for quantum sensing, simulation, and error correction using programmable atom arrays and other platforms.

faculty.name: Nikta Fakhri
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Fakhri leads the Physics of Living Systems group, studying active, non-equilibrium matter from molecules to cells to whole organisms. Her group uses starfish oocytes and embryos as model systems to reveal how microscopic, energy-consuming fluctuations produce macroscopic symmetry breaking, including odd elasticity and chiral 'active crystals' of spinning embryos. She pioneered fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotubes as probes of intracellular mechanics and develops theoretical frameworks connecting broken time-reversal symmetry, entropy production, and pattern formation in living matter.

faculty.name: Anna Frebel
faculty.title: Professor of Physics, Division Head for Astrophysics
faculty.summary: Frebel practices 'stellar archaeology,' using high-resolution spectroscopy of the oldest, most metal-poor stars in the Milky Way halo and nearby dwarf galaxies to reconstruct chemical conditions in the early universe. Her measurements of individual stars over 12 billion years old constrain the first stars, supernova nucleosynthesis yields, and the r-process that forges heavy elements. Recent work characterizes r-process-enhanced stars and dwarf-galaxy chemical evolution, probing early star formation and the assembly history of the Galaxy and its satellites.

faculty.name: Liang Fu
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Fu is a condensed matter theorist known for co-predicting topological crystalline insulators and advancing the theory of topological superconductivity and Majorana states. His recent work explores fractional quantum anomalous Hall states and correlated phases in moire and twisted transition-metal-dichalcogenide bilayers, the role of quantum geometry and Berry curvature in flat-band physics, and unconventional superconductivity. He also develops ideas connecting band topology, ferroelectricity, and nonlinear optical and transport responses in quantum materials for potential device applications.

faculty.name: Joseph Checkelsky
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Checkelsky's group synthesizes and measures crystalline quantum materials to uncover exotic electronic states arising from topology and geometric frustration. His team pioneered kagome-lattice metals hosting flat electronic bands and recently reported realizing a flat band in a three-dimensional crystal, a route toward strong correlations and possible superconductivity. Recent work includes evidence of striped electronic phases in structurally modulated superlattices and studies linking flat bands, strange-metal behavior, and the Kondo effect, aiming to engineer materials with tailored quantum functionality.

faculty.name: Martin Zwierlein
faculty.title: Thomas A. Frank (1977) Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Zwierlein studies strongly interacting ultracold Fermi gases as tunable analogs of complex quantum matter. His group uses quantum gas microscopy to image individual fermionic atoms in optical lattices, probing Hubbard-model physics, pairing, and transport relevant to high-temperature superconductivity. Recent efforts include creating and cooling ultracold polar molecules toward molecular quantum computing and simulation, measuring universal thermodynamics and spin transport in unitary Fermi gases, and directly visualizing pairing and magnetic correlations to test theories of correlated fermion systems.

faculty.name: Vladan Vuletic
faculty.title: Lester Wolfe Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Vuletic works at the interface of quantum optics and quantum information with atoms and light. His group generates spin-squeezed and entangled atomic ensembles to push optical atomic clocks and sensors beyond the standard quantum limit. Working with neutral-atom and Rydberg-array platforms, his collaborations have demonstrated programmable quantum simulators and, more recently, logical qubits and error-corrected operations in reconfigurable atom arrays. He also studies strong photon-photon interactions mediated by atoms, building toward scalable quantum networks and computation.

faculty.name: Sara Seager
faculty.title: Professor of Physics; Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics; Class of 1941 Professor of Planetary Science
faculty.summary: Seager is a leader in exoplanet science, pioneering theory of exoplanet atmospheres, interiors, and the search for signs of life on worlds beyond the Solar System. She develops frameworks for identifying atmospheric biosignature gases and studies how starlight interacts with planetary atmospheres. She has led and contributed to space missions including TESS and small satellites, and continues designing concepts for directly imaging Earth-like planets. Recent work spans candidate biosignatures, exoplanet atmosphere characterization, and instrumentation for future missions to detect habitable environments.

faculty.name: Erin Kara
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Kara is an observational astrophysicist studying how black holes grow and shape their surroundings. She advanced X-ray reverberation mapping, using time lags between X-ray light echoes off infalling gas to map accretion flows and probe strongly curved spacetime near event horizons. Her group analyzes black hole X-ray binaries, active galactic nuclei, tidal disruption events, and quasi-periodic eruptions using missions such as NICER. She serves as deputy principal investigator for the proposed AXIS X-ray probe mission concept targeting a 2030s launch.

faculty.name: Iain Stewart
faculty.title: Otto (1939) and Jane Morningstar Professor of Science
faculty.summary: Stewart is a theoretical particle physicist and a founder of soft-collinear effective theory, a framework for describing energetic quarks and gluons in QCD. His research develops precision predictions for collider observables at the LHC, including jet substructure, event shapes, and factorization theorems that separate physics across energy scales. Recent work refines high-precision extractions of Standard Model parameters such as the strong coupling and top-quark mass, and applies effective field theory methods to jets, resummation, and searches for new physics.

faculty.name: Phiala Shanahan
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Shanahan is a nuclear and particle theorist using lattice quantum chromodynamics to compute the structure of hadrons and light nuclei from first principles. She pioneers physics-informed machine learning, embedding gauge and spacetime symmetries into generative models to sample high-dimensional lattice field-theory distributions far more efficiently than conventional methods. Her group achieved early calculations of the gluon structure of nuclei and studies the origin of nuclear properties, supporting interpretation of experiments at facilities including the future Electron-Ion Collider.

faculty.name: Janet Conrad
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Conrad is an experimental particle physicist searching for new physics in the neutrino sector. She has led and contributed to accelerator and detector experiments including MiniBooNE, MicroBooNE, and IceCube, investigating anomalies that could signal light sterile neutrinos beyond the three known flavors. Her group develops the IsoDAR concept, a compact cyclotron-driven decay-at-rest source designed for definitive sterile-neutrino and precision electroweak searches. Recent work spans global fits to oscillation data, detector R&D, and interpretation of persistent short-baseline anomalies.

faculty.name: William Oliver
faculty.title: Professor of Physics; Henry Ellis Warren (1894) Professor of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science
faculty.summary: Oliver leads MIT's Engineering Quantum Systems group, developing superconducting qubits and the materials, fabrication, and control systems needed for scalable quantum processors. His team studies coherence-limiting noise, high-fidelity gates, and architectures for modular and chiplet-based quantum computers. Recent work demonstrated commensurate-pulse control that suppresses counter-rotating errors in low-frequency fluxonium qubits, enabling faster, more accurate operations, and advanced fast, high-fidelity two-qubit gates. He also directs MIT quantum-engineering initiatives bridging campus and Lincoln Laboratory toward fault-tolerant quantum computing.

faculty.name: Jeff Gore
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Gore applies quantitative experimental and theoretical physics to living systems, using laboratory microbial communities as tractable models of ecology and evolution. His group tests theories of population dynamics, cooperation, and cheating, mapping how interactions produce stability, tipping points, and early-warning signals before ecosystem collapse. Recent work explores how diversity, coexistence, and emergent phases arise in communities of many species, drawing analogies to statistical-physics phase transitions and probing rules that govern assembly, resilience, and evolutionary dynamics in complex biological systems.

faculty.name: Miklos Porkolab
faculty.title: Professor of Physics, Post-Tenure
faculty.summary: Porkolab is a plasma physicist focused on magnetically confined fusion and the fundamental physics of hot plasmas. He founded and long directed MIT's Plasma Science and Fusion Center research on wave-plasma interactions, radio-frequency heating and current drive, parametric instabilities, and turbulence-driven transport in tokamaks. His group develops advanced microwave and laser scattering diagnostics to measure plasma fluctuations, validating turbulence models on devices such as Alcator C-Mod and DIII-D and informing the physics basis for burning-plasma experiments including ITER and compact high-field fusion concepts.

requirements.english: IELTS Academic 7.0 overall (min 7.0 listening/writing/speaking, 6.5 reading) or TOEFL iBT 100 (min 25 in each element); some English-medium academic backgrounds may be exempt.
requirements.admission_model_note: Applicants apply to a specific research group and a named supervisor must agree to supervise before an offer is made; identifying and contacting a prospective supervisor beforehand is encouraged.

funding.note: Funding is competitive via scholarships (Cambridge Trust, Gates Cambridge, EPSRC/STFC studentships, and Cavendish/college awards); one application is considered for most schemes but funding is not guaranteed.

requirements.contact_note: Applicants are encouraged to identify and contact a prospective supervisor or research group before applying.

faculty.name: Mete Atatüre
faculty.title: Professor of Physics (Head of Department)
faculty.summary: Atatüre leads the Quantum Information and Control group and is Head of the Cavendish. His experimental work builds light-matter quantum interfaces in solids for quantum-network hardware, using single spins in semiconductor quantum dots and colour centres in diamond and hexagonal boron nitride. Recent efforts focus on optically active spin qubits and single-photon sources in atomically thin 2D materials and van der Waals heterostructures, nanoscale quantum sensing and imaging, and generating spin-photon entanglement for distributed quantum communication.

faculty.name: Suchitra Sebastian
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Sebastian leads the Fundamental Physics of Quantum Matter group, creating and probing complex correlated materials under extreme conditions of ultra-low temperature, intense magnetic fields, and high pressure. Using quantum-oscillation and transport measurements, her recent work has uncovered an unconventional insulating state that unexpectedly displays metal-like quantum oscillations, explored unconventional and high-temperature superconductivity, and searched for exotic emergent phases in Kondo insulators and strongly correlated electron systems. She was awarded the 2022 New Horizons in Physics Prize for this research.

faculty.name: Claudio Castelnovo
faculty.title: Professor of Theoretical Physics
faculty.summary: Castelnovo is a Professor of Theoretical Physics in the Theory of Condensed Matter group. His research addresses emergent and out-of-equilibrium phenomena in strongly correlated many-body systems, notably frustrated magnets and spin ice, where he helped predict emergent magnetic monopoles. Recent work spans the slow relaxation and dynamics of spin-ice materials, fractionalised excitations and topological order, quantum spin liquids, and how disorder and non-equilibrium driving shape quantum matter, increasingly connecting these ideas to quantum simulation and quantum information.

faculty.name: Roberto Maiolino
faculty.title: Professor of Experimental Astrophysics
faculty.summary: Maiolino is Professor of Experimental Astrophysics and a Royal Society Research Professor studying galaxy formation and the growth of supermassive black holes in the early Universe. He co-leads JWST-NIRSpec guaranteed-time surveys and has recently reported some of the earliest and most distant galaxies and black holes yet found, including candidate direct-collapse black holes and unexpectedly chemically enriched primordial systems. His group also works on galaxy chemical evolution, outflows and star-formation quenching, and instrumentation for JWST, the VLT and the ELT.

faculty.name: Paula Álvarez Cartelle
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Álvarez Cartelle is an Associate Professor in the High Energy Physics group and a member of the LHCb collaboration at CERN's Large Hadron Collider. Her research centres on precision tests of the Standard Model through rare b-quark decays, particularly b to s(d) dilepton transitions that probe lepton-flavour universality and potential new physics. She led Cambridge's LHCb team from 2022 to 2025 and works on flavour-physics measurements and detector operations exploiting the high-statistics datasets delivered by LHC Run 3.

faculty.name: Akshay Rao
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Rao is a Professor of Physics leading optoelectronics and energy-materials research at the Cavendish and Maxwell Centre. His group uses ultrafast optical spectroscopy and microscopy to map electronic, structural and transport dynamics in organic semiconductors, metal-halide perovskites and other energy materials with high spatial and temporal precision. Recent work includes singlet fission for enhanced photovoltaics, long-range triplet and charge transport, and photon multiplication; he co-founded Cambridge Photon Technology and Illumion to commercialise these advances.

faculty.name: Sarah Bohndiek
faculty.title: Professor of Biomedical Physics
faculty.summary: Bohndiek is Professor of Biomedical Physics, jointly appointed with the CRUK Cambridge Institute, and leads the VISION lab within the Physics of Life theme. Her group develops low-cost optics and photonics for early cancer detection, especially photoacoustic imaging and hyperspectral endoscopy to interrogate tumour vasculature, oxygenation and the microenvironment. Recent efforts translate these methods toward the clinic, including endoscopic detection of oesophageal disease, and use imaging to track tumour evolution and metabolic changes during cancer progression.

faculty.name: Jeremy Baumberg
faculty.title: Harold Aspden Professor of Fundamental Physics
faculty.summary: Baumberg, the Harold Aspden Professor of Fundamental Physics, directs the NanoPhotonics Centre and the NanoDTC. His group studies strong light-matter coupling at the nanoscale, using plasmonic nanostructures and 'picocavities' that confine light to sub-nanometre volumes for single-molecule and single-atom spectroscopy and chemistry. Recent work spans molecular optomechanics, real-time SERS tracking of chemical reactions, tunable nanoparticle-on-mirror architectures, self-assembled metamaterials, and scalable approaches to nanophotonic devices, bridging fundamental optics with applications in sensing, catalysis and energy.

faculty.name: Siân Dutton
faculty.title: Professor of Physics and Solid-State Chemistry
faculty.summary: Dutton is a Professor of Physics and Solid-State Chemistry in the Quantum Matter group and Director of the Winton Programme for the Physics of Sustainability. Her multidisciplinary group synthesises and characterises functional inorganic materials, linking composition and structure to physical properties. Recent research targets energy materials, including new cathodes for lithium- and sodium-ion batteries, alongside geometrically frustrated magnets, hybrid perovskites, and low-temperature magnetocaloric materials for magnetic refrigeration, combining crystal chemistry with magnetometry, neutron scattering and low-temperature measurements.

faculty.name: Nicola Marzari
faculty.title: Cavendish Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Marzari, elected Cavendish Professor of Physics, is a computational condensed-matter physicist developing electronic-structure theory, transport methods, and data-driven materials discovery. His group builds first-principles quantum-mechanical simulations to predict and design the properties of complex materials, pioneering maximally-localized Wannier functions and open infrastructures such as Quantum ESPRESSO, AiiDA, and the Materials Cloud. Recent work integrates machine learning and AI into high-throughput screening for energy materials, topological insulators, and two-dimensional systems. He received the 2026 APS David Adler Award in Materials Physics.

faculty.name: Henning Sirringhaus
faculty.title: Hitachi Professor of Electron Device Physics
faculty.summary: Sirringhaus, Hitachi Professor of Electron Device Physics and a Royal Society Research Professor, leads the Optoelectronics group studying charge and spin transport and photophysics of organic and solution-processed inorganic semiconductors, including halide perovskites. Recent work uses field-induced electron spin resonance to probe spin-charge dynamics, and examines non-equilibrium transport in polymer mixed ionic-electronic conductors at ultrahigh charge densities. His group targets spin relaxation, spin-to-charge conversion, and doping strategies that improve organic semiconductor performance for flexible electronics, spintronics, and bioelectronic devices.

faculty.name: Zoran Hadzibabic
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Hadzibabic leads an AMOP experimental group using ultracold nanokelvin quantum gases in optical box traps as clean, homogeneous platforms for many-body physics. Interests span superfluidity, reduced dimensionality, and far-from-equilibrium dynamics. Recent Nature work established a universal equation of state for wave turbulence in a quantum gas, alongside studies of turbulent cascades, driven disordered gases, and universal coarsening. The box-trap approach simplifies comparison with theory and enables qualitatively new experiments on non-equilibrium phenomena that remain hard to understand elsewhere.

faculty.name: Ulrich Keyser
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Keyser, Professor of Applied Physics, works at the interface of physics, chemistry, and the life sciences, developing single-molecule sensing with solid-state and DNA nanopores. His group engineers digitally encoded DNA nanostructures and dumbbell nanoswitches for highly multiplexed detection of nucleic acids, proteins, and bacterial identification, integrable into small, portable devices. Recent work advances DNA nanopore design, self-assembled molecular sensors, and optical-tweezers control of DNA translocation. He also serves as Director of Graduate Education at the Cavendish Laboratory.

faculty.name: Eugene Terentjev
faculty.title: Professor of Polymer Physics
faculty.summary: Terentjev, Professor of Polymer Physics, studies the structure and dynamics of complex soft materials, including polymers, liquid crystals, and colloids, with growing impact on molecular and cell biophysics. He is a foundational figure in liquid crystal elastomers, co-authoring the standard monograph with the late Mark Warner. Recent work covers liquid-crystal elastomer actuators and sensors, mechanobiology of the cytoskeleton, protein folding, and self-assembly. His group couples theory with a purpose-built chemistry lab to advance stimuli-responsive and programmable materials.

faculty.name: Russell Cowburn
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Cowburn FRS returned to Cambridge in 2010 and researches nanoscale magnetism and spintronics, pioneering three-dimensional nanomagnetic systems. His group studies magnetic solitons and domain walls in multilayered synthetic antiferromagnets as data carriers for vertical, 3D data transfer, plus interconnected magnetic nanowire networks as candidate multistate memristors. Applications target low-energy computing, ultrahigh-density storage, and healthcare devices. A prolific inventor with many patents and start-ups, he bridges fundamental magnetization dynamics with commercial nanotechnology and anti-counterfeiting security.

faculty.name: Sandro Tacchella
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Extragalactic Astrophysics
faculty.summary: Tacchella, Assistant Professor of Extragalactic Astrophysics and a member of the Kavli Institute for Cosmology, investigates how the first galaxies formed, how bulges and disks assemble, and how star formation shuts down in massive galaxies. He plays a key role in JWST NIRCam data processing and early-universe surveys. Recent 2025 work analyzing hundreds of low-mass galaxies shows early galaxies were turbulent and dynamically unsettled, growing through frequent mergers and bursts of star formation rather than smooth, orderly assembly.

faculty.name: Eloy de Lera Acedo
faculty.title: Professor of Radioastronomy
faculty.summary: De Lera Acedo, Professor of Radioastronomy, heads Cavendish Radio Astronomy and Cosmology, studying the infant Universe through the redshifted hyperfine 21-cm line from primordial hydrogen. He is international PI of the REACH experiment and the CosmoCube space mission, targeting the Dark Ages, Cosmic Dawn, and Epoch of Reionization. He leads Cambridge SKA development activities and sits on the HERA executive board. Recent work advances antenna design, foreground modeling, and receiver calibration needed to extract these extremely faint cosmological signals.

faculty.name: Tina Potter
faculty.title: Professor of High Energy Physics
faculty.summary: Potter, Professor of High Energy Physics, searches for physics beyond the Standard Model using the ATLAS detector at the LHC. Her research focuses on dark matter and supersymmetry, hunting for new particles produced in high-energy proton collisions and developing novel analysis techniques to isolate subtle signatures from overwhelming Standard Model backgrounds. She holds ATLAS experiment leadership responsibilities within the Cambridge group. Her work combines collider phenomenology, detector understanding, and statistical methods to constrain candidate explanations for dark matter.

faculty.name: Crispin Barnes
faculty.title: Professor of Quantum Physics
faculty.summary: Barnes, Professor of Quantum Physics, leads the Thin Film Magnetism and Quantum Information groups, with interests spanning quantum information theory, spintronics, materials science, and beyond. He operates bespoke molecular-beam-epitaxy, cleanroom, and low-temperature facilities to grow and measure magnetic oxides, topological insulators, and quantum devices. Recent work addresses fast, pulse-based state preparation in silicon spin qubits, deriving minimal evolution times for high-fidelity control. His eclectic program also touches environmental physics and financial modeling alongside solid-state quantum device engineering.

faculty.name: Anton Souslov
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Souslov, Associate Professor in the Theory of Condensed Matter group, develops theories of soft and active materials, mechanical metamaterials, topological states, and polymer physics. He combines motors with elastic elements to describe self-driven, shape-changing matter, and uses topological band theory to design exotic classical waves, including the first optical fiber guiding light via a topological state. Recent work explores topological active matter, odd elasticity, and non-reciprocal mechanics, revealing robust edge modes and unusual responses in driven, out-of-equilibrium systems.

faculty.name: Didier Queloz
faculty.title: Jacksonian Professor of Natural Philosophy
faculty.summary: Queloz, Jacksonian Professor of Natural Philosophy and 2019 Nobel laureate for the first detection of an exoplanet around a Sun-like star, now leads efforts to detect Earth-like planets and assess their habitability. He directs the Leverhulme Centre for Life in the Universe, integrating astrophysics, chemistry, and biology to study the origins and prevalence of life. Recent work advances radial-velocity and transit instrumentation, atmospheric characterization of small planets, and interdisciplinary programs connecting exoplanet discovery to prebiotic chemistry and biosignatures.

programs.university: ETH Zürich (Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Zurich)
programs.program_name: Doctorate in Physics (Dr. sc. ETH Zürich)

requirements.deadline_display: Rolling — no fixed deadline (apply once a supervising professor offers a position)
requirements.fee_display: No formal application fee (one-time doctoral tuition ~CHF 1,500)
requirements.english: No standard department-level English test required; English proficiency and language of supervision are assessed by the hiring professor — verify.
requirements.admission_model_note: You must first secure a professor at ETH Zürich willing to offer you a doctoral position; applications go directly to individual research groups and admission follows from that arrangement.

funding.note: Doctoral researchers are employed with a salary (standard gross ~CHF 61,300 in year 1, rising annually) rather than paying tuition beyond a one-time ~CHF 1,500 fee.

requirements.contact_note: Contacting a prospective supervisor is effectively required — you must find a professor willing to offer a position before you can be admitted.

faculty.name: Andreas Wallraff
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Wallraff's Quantum Device Lab builds superconducting circuits for quantum computing and communication. Recent work demonstrated repeated real-time quantum error correction with a distance-three surface code on a 17-qubit processor, fast feedback and leakage mitigation, and deterministic microwave-photon state transfer and remote entanglement between cryogenically separated nodes over a cryogenic link. The group also develops hybrid systems coupling superconducting circuits to semiconductor spin qubits and Rydberg atoms, advancing scalable error-corrected processors and modular quantum networks.

faculty.name: Jonathan Home
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Home's Trapped Ion Quantum Information group encodes and controls quantum information in the motional and internal states of trapped ions. Recent work realized and stabilized Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill bosonic error-correcting codes, using dissipative and feedback schemes to extend logical coherence, and demonstrated real-time correction of oscillator errors that lengthened coherence times. The group also pursues scalable architectures with integrated photonics for ion addressing, multi-zone traps, and mixed-species operations, pushing toward fault-tolerant trapped-ion quantum computation.

faculty.name: Tilman Esslinger
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Esslinger's Quantum Optics group uses ultracold fermionic and bosonic atoms in optical lattices to simulate strongly correlated many-body physics. Recent experiments studied transport and conductance in mesoscopic atomic channels, engineered dynamical and Floquet lattices, and realized geometric two-qubit gates using doublon states of fermionic atoms. The group also couples atomic gases to optical cavities to explore cavity-mediated interactions, self-organization, dissipative and supersolid phases, and probes quantum magnetism and Fermi-Hubbard physics in tunable synthetic quantum matter.

faculty.name: Jérôme Faist
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Faist's Quantum Optoelectronics group pioneers quantum cascade lasers and intersubband devices. Recent work developed broadband terahertz and mid-infrared frequency combs and dual-comb spectroscopy sources, including surface-emitting THz combs with inverse-designed integrated reflectors and frequency-modulated combs in field-enhancing tapered waveguides. The group studies cavity and ultrastrong light-matter coupling, engineers high-power octave-spanning emitters, and explores non-classical light generation and quantum sensing with intersubband systems, advancing compact coherent sources spanning the mid-infrared to terahertz range.

faculty.name: Rainer Wallny
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Wallny leads an experimental high-energy physics group on the CMS experiment at CERN's LHC and heads the Institute for Particle Physics and Astrophysics. Recent activity centers on precision characterization of the Higgs boson, especially in diphoton and b-quark final states, searches for supersymmetry and physics beyond the Standard Model, and top-quark physics. The group leads substantial detector R&D, developing and characterizing silicon pixel and tracking sensors and readout electronics for the CMS upgrade toward the High-Luminosity LHC.

faculty.name: Alexandre Refregier
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Refregier's Cosmology group studies dark matter, dark energy and cosmic structure formation using weak gravitational lensing and large surveys. Recent work delivered cosmological constraints from the Dark Energy Survey and developed forward-modeling and simulation-based inference pipelines, combined-probe analyses, and machine-learning methods for cosmic shear and dark-matter mass mapping. The group builds fast simulation and baryonification frameworks to control systematics and prepares analyses for upcoming surveys, testing the standard cosmological model and the validity of general relativity on cosmological scales.

faculty.name: Renato Renner
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Renner heads the Quantum Information Theory group, working on the foundations of quantum mechanics, quantum cryptography and quantum thermodynamics. Recent contributions include results on the generalized quantum Stein's lemma and the second law, finite-size and one-shot information theory, and conceptual analyses of what a quantum state represents and how observers consistently describe one another, extending Wigner's-friend-type arguments. The group also develops security proofs for quantum key distribution and applies information-theoretic and machine-learning tools at the interface of thermodynamics and information.

faculty.name: Eugene Demler
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Demler's group develops theory for strongly correlated and out-of-equilibrium quantum many-body systems, closely tied to quantum-simulation experiments. Recent work interprets and guides ultracold-atom and superconducting-qubit simulators, studies controlled dissipation as a resource for engineering correlated states, and analyzes doped Hubbard systems, magnetic polarons and their detection via quantum-gas microscopy. The group applies machine learning to many-body data, explores cavity-QED control of materials and quantum optics of solids, and models driven, nonequilibrium quantum matter.

faculty.name: Gabriel Aeppli
faculty.title: Full Professor
faculty.summary: Aeppli's Quantum Technologies group, linked to PSI's photon science division, studies quantum materials and solid-state qubit platforms using X-ray, neutron and synchrotron techniques. Recent work probed kagome magnets such as Fe3Sn2 and their topological electronic and spin states via resonant inelastic X-ray scattering and X-ray-detected ferromagnetic resonance at SwissFEL, and demonstrated highly coherent two-level systems relevant to solid-state qubits. The group also develops silicon donor-based and rare-earth qubit platforms and helped establish the ETH-PSI Quantum Computing Hub.

faculty.name: Klaus Ensslin
faculty.title: Full Professor, Laboratory for Solid State Physics
faculty.summary: Ensslin is a full professor in the Laboratory for Solid State Physics, leading the Nanophysics group. His experimental research probes quantum transport in nanostructures, including quantum dots, graphene and other two-dimensional materials, gallium arsenide heterostructures, and hybrid superconductor-semiconductor devices. The group studies electron confinement, spin qubits, and topological states for quantum information, using low-temperature and high-magnetic-field measurements to reveal the electronic properties of engineered mesoscopic systems.

faculty.name: Christian Degen
faculty.title: Full Professor, Laboratory for Solid State Physics
faculty.summary: Degen is a full professor in the Laboratory for Solid State Physics, leading the Spin Physics group. His research develops ultrasensitive quantum sensing based on nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond and scanning-probe techniques, aiming at nanoscale magnetic resonance imaging of individual molecules and spins. The group also explores nanomechanical resonators and precision measurements, pushing the spatial resolution and sensitivity of magnetometry toward single-spin detection for applications in condensed matter physics, chemistry, and the life sciences.

faculty.name: Andrey Zheludev
faculty.title: Full Professor, Laboratory for Solid State Physics
faculty.summary: Zheludev is a full professor in the Laboratory for Solid State Physics and heads the group for neutron scattering and magnetism. His research investigates quantum magnetism and novel states of matter in low-dimensional and frustrated magnetic materials, using neutron scattering, high magnetic fields, and thermodynamic probes. Central topics include quantum phase transitions, magnetic Bose-Einstein condensation, spin liquids, and emergent quasiparticles, combining single-crystal synthesis with experiments at international neutron and X-ray facilities.

faculty.name: Atac Imamoglu
faculty.title: Full Professor, Institute for Quantum Electronics
faculty.summary: Imamoglu is a full professor at the Institute for Quantum Electronics, leading the Quantum Photonics group at the interface of quantum optics and condensed matter. His research explores light-matter interaction in solid-state systems, including semiconductor quantum dots, exciton-polaritons, and two-dimensional and moire materials. The group studies strongly correlated electrons probed optically, single-photon sources, and cavity quantum electrodynamics, developing new platforms where photons and electronic excitations reveal and control quantum many-body phenomena.

faculty.name: Rachel Grange
faculty.title: Professor, Institute for Quantum Electronics
faculty.summary: Grange is a professor at the Institute for Quantum Electronics, leading the Optical Nanomaterial Group. Her research engineers nonlinear and electro-optic nanomaterials, such as barium titanate and lithium niobate nanostructures, for photonic and quantum devices. The group develops second-harmonic imaging, metasurfaces, and integrated photonics, exploiting nanoscale light-matter interaction for frequency conversion, sensing, and reconfigurable optics. Her work bridges materials science and nanophotonics to create compact components for classical and quantum information technologies.

faculty.name: Günther Dissertori
faculty.title: Full Professor, Institute for Particle Physics and Astrophysics
faculty.summary: Dissertori is a full professor of particle physics at the Institute for Particle Physics and Astrophysics and serves in ETH Zurich's leadership as Rector. His experimental group works on the CMS experiment at CERN's Large Hadron Collider, contributing to the discovery and precision study of the Higgs boson, tests of the Standard Model, and searches for new physics. He also develops advanced calorimetry and detector technologies, including applications of scintillating crystals in medical imaging.

faculty.name: Didier Queloz
faculty.title: Full Professor, Institute for Particle Physics and Astrophysics
faculty.summary: Queloz is a full professor at the Institute for Particle Physics and Astrophysics and a 2019 Nobel laureate in Physics for the first discovery of an exoplanet orbiting a Sun-like star. His group studies the detection and characterization of extrasolar planets and the conditions for planetary habitability. He founded and directs ETH's Centre for the Origin and Prevalence of Life, pursuing interdisciplinary research on how life emerges and how it might be detected beyond Earth.

faculty.name: Sascha Quanz
faculty.title: Associate Professor, Institute for Particle Physics and Astrophysics
faculty.summary: Quanz is an associate professor at the Institute for Particle Physics and Astrophysics, leading the Exoplanets and Habitability group. His research targets the direct imaging and characterization of extrasolar planets, searching for atmospheric biosignatures on potentially habitable worlds. The group develops observational techniques and instruments for large ground- and space-based telescopes, and he is principal investigator of LIFE, a proposed space mission using mid-infrared nulling interferometry to study nearby terrestrial exoplanets.

faculty.name: André Rubbia
faculty.title: Full Professor, Institute for Particle Physics and Astrophysics
faculty.summary: Rubbia is a full professor at the Institute for Particle Physics and Astrophysics, leading a research group in neutrino and astroparticle physics. He pioneered large liquid-argon time-projection chamber detectors and plays a leading role in international long-baseline neutrino experiments such as DUNE. His group investigates neutrino oscillations, matter-antimatter asymmetry, and searches for exotic and dark matter, developing novel detector technologies to probe fundamental questions about the nature and interactions of neutrinos.

faculty.name: Matthias Gaberdiel
faculty.title: Full Professor, Institute for Theoretical Physics
faculty.summary: Gaberdiel is a full professor at the Institute for Theoretical Physics, working on string theory and two-dimensional conformal field theory. His research focuses on the AdS/CFT correspondence, higher-spin symmetries, and exactly solvable string backgrounds, seeking to understand the mathematical structure of quantum gravity and holography. He has contributed foundational results on conformal field theories, vertex operator algebras, and the tensionless limit of string theory, deepening the connection between gauge theories and gravitational dualities.

faculty.name: Gian Michele Graf
faculty.title: Full Professor, Institute for Theoretical Physics
faculty.summary: Graf is a full professor at the Institute for Theoretical Physics, working in mathematical physics. His research applies rigorous mathematics to condensed matter and quantum theory, including topological insulators, the quantum Hall effect, transport in mesoscopic systems, and open quantum systems. He has also studied the foundations of thermodynamics, many-body scattering, and quantum pumps, providing exact results that clarify the physical origin of topological invariants and the stability of quantized responses in matter.

faculty.name: Yiwen Chu
faculty.title: Associate Professor, Laboratory for Solid State Physics
faculty.summary: Chu is an associate professor and head of the Laboratory for Solid State Physics, leading the Hybrid Quantum Systems group. Her research couples superconducting circuits to high-frequency mechanical resonators, creating quantum acoustic devices that store and manipulate quantum information in sound. The group explores circuit quantum acoustodynamics, mechanical quantum memories, and interfaces between disparate quantum platforms, aiming to harness massive acoustic modes as a resource for quantum computing, sensing, and tests of macroscopic quantum mechanics.

faculty.title: Professor; MCS Associate Dean for Research
faculty.summary: Experimental high-energy physics at the CMS experiment (LHC). Higgs boson physics, electroweak measurements, and detector development.

faculty.name: Colin Morningstar
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical nuclear/particle physics. Lattice QCD calculations of hadron spectroscopy. Exotic hadrons and resonances from first-principles QCD.

faculty.name: Jeffrey Peterson
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Observational cosmology with 21cm radio astronomy. Probing the epoch of reionization and cosmic dawn through neutral hydrogen surveys.

faculty.name: Ira Rothstein
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical high energy physics. Effective field theories for QCD, heavy quarks, and gravitational-wave physics. Connections to collider phenomenology.

faculty.name: Matthew Walker
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Observational astrophysics of dwarf galaxies. Using stellar kinematics of dwarf spheroidals to constrain dark matter distribution and galaxy formation.

faculty.name: Michael Widom
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical condensed matter physics. Electronic structure of complex materials, quasicrystals, metallic alloys, and computational modeling of materials.

faculty.name: Roy Briere
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental high-energy physics. Charm physics, CP violation studies, and tau lepton physics with the BESIII experiment.

faculty.name: Sara Majetich
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental condensed matter physics. Magnetic nanoparticles, nanomagnetism, and spintronics. Synthesis and characterization of magnetic nanomaterials.

faculty.name: Markus Deserno
faculty.title: Professor; Director of Graduate Affairs
faculty.summary: Theoretical biological physics. Computational modeling of lipid membranes, membrane-protein interactions, and coarse-grained simulations of soft biological matter.

faculty.name: John Alison
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental high-energy physics at ATLAS (LHC). Higgs boson physics, top quark studies, and machine learning for particle physics.

faculty.name: Benjamin Hunt
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental condensed matter. Quantum transport in 2D materials and van der Waals heterostructures. Mesoscopic physics and correlated electron systems.

faculty.name: Jyoti Katoch
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental condensed matter. ARPES studies of electronic structure in 2D materials and quantum materials. Strongly correlated and topological systems.

faculty.name: Diana Parno
faculty.title: Falco-DeBenedetti Associate Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Experimental neutrino physics. Direct neutrino mass measurement with KATRIN experiment. Neutrino properties and their cosmological implications.

faculty.name: Riccardo Penco
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical high energy physics. Effective field theory methods for particle physics and cosmology. Scattering amplitudes and cosmological correlators.

faculty.name: Rachel Rosen
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical physics at the intersection of quantum field theory, gravity, and cosmology. Effective field theory techniques for gravitational physics.

faculty.name: Sufei Shi
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental condensed matter. Quantum transport and optoelectronic properties of 2D materials and their heterostructures.

faculty.name: Simranjeet Singh
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental condensed matter. Spintronics and spin transport in quantum materials. 2D magnetic materials and spin-orbit coupling phenomena.

faculty.name: Hy Trac
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Computational cosmology. Numerical simulations of large-scale structure, galaxy clusters, and cosmic reionization. Hydrodynamical simulations of structure formation.

faculty.name: Katelyn Breivik
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Computational astrophysics. Binary star evolution and population synthesis. Predicting gravitational wave source populations for LIGO/Virgo and LISA.

faculty.name: Shubhayu Chatterjee
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Theory of topological phases of matter and strongly correlated electron systems.

faculty.name: Matteo Cremonesi
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental particle physics. Neutrino physics with DUNE experiment. Liquid argon time projection chamber technology.

faculty.name: Valentina Dutta
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental particle physics at CMS (LHC). Precision Higgs measurements and searches for new physics.

faculty.name: Vladyslav Kozii
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical condensed matter physics. Topological materials, superconductivity, and quantum transport in Dirac and Weyl semimetals.

faculty.name: Antonella Palmese
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Observational cosmology. Dark energy studies with gravitational wave standard sirens and large-scale structure surveys including DES and LSST.

faculty.name: Fangwei Si
faculty.title: Cooper-Siegel Assistant Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Experimental biological physics. Quantitative studies of bacterial cell physiology. Single-cell dynamics, growth control, and cellular decision-making.

faculty.name: Grigory Tarnopolskiy
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical physics spanning condensed matter and high energy theory. Quantum field theory methods applied to condensed matter systems.

funding.note: Fully funded (GSAS fellowship: tuition, fees, stipend)

requirements.admission_model: Direct admission to department; all admits assumed PhD candidates

funding.note: Fully funded (GSAS fellowship: tuition, fees, stipend)

faculty.name: Brian Greene
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics and Physics
faculty.summary: World-famous theoretical physicist and co-founder of the World Science Festival. Major contributions to string theory, mirror symmetry, and Calabi-Yau compactifications. Bestselling author of 'The Elegant Universe' and multiple other popular science books.

faculty.name: Elena Aprile
faculty.title: Centennial Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Founder and spokesperson of the XENON dark matter search experiment, which has set the world's most stringent limits on WIMP dark matter. Her work has defined the field of direct dark matter detection using liquid xenon time projection chambers.

faculty.name: Dmitri N. Basov
faculty.title: Higgins Professor of Physics; Department Chair
faculty.summary: Leading experimentalist in quantum materials, pioneering infrared nano-spectroscopy and nano-imaging techniques to probe exotic phases in correlated electron systems, topological materials, and van der Waals heterostructures. Highly Cited Researcher.

faculty.name: Brian David Metzger
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: MacArthur Fellow and Blavatnik Award winner (2019). Predicted kilonova emission from neutron star mergers before the 2017 LIGO-Virgo-GW170817 event. Leading theorist of multi-messenger astrophysics and transient phenomena.

faculty.name: William Allen Zajc
faculty.title: I. I. Rabi Professor of Physics
faculty.sub_field: High Energy Experiment / Heavy Ion Physics
faculty.summary: World-leading experimentalist in relativistic heavy ion physics. Key leader of the PHENIX and sPHENIX experiments at RHIC, studying the quark-gluon plasma. Former Department Chair and member of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences.

faculty.name: Szabolcs Marka
faculty.title: Walter O. LeCroy, Jr. Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Key contributor to LIGO and multi-messenger astronomy. His group played a central role in the first gravitational wave detections. Research spans gravitational waves, gamma-ray bursts, and the IceCube neutrino observatory.

faculty.name: Andrew J. Millis
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Prominent condensed matter theorist who co-developed the dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) approach for strongly correlated electron systems. Major contributions to understanding high-temperature superconductivity and metal-insulator transitions.

faculty.name: Alfred H. Mueller
faculty.title: Enrico Fermi Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Eminent QCD theorist. Pioneered the theory of parton saturation and the Color Glass Condensate, which describes the high-density regime of QCD probed at RHIC and the LHC. Recipient of the Sakurai Prize (2003). Member of the National Academy of Sciences.

faculty.name: Angela Olinto
faculty.title: Provost of Columbia University; Professor of Astronomy and Physics
faculty.summary: Currently Provost of Columbia University. Leading researcher in ultra-high-energy cosmic rays and neutrino astrophysics. Principal investigator for the POEMMA space mission concept and key contributor to the Pierre Auger Observatory.

faculty.name: Norman H. Christ
faculty.title: Ephraim Gildor Professor of Computational Theoretical Physics
faculty.summary: Leader in lattice QCD calculations. Has led the RBC-UKQCD collaboration in precision computations of kaon decay amplitudes relevant to CP violation and the Standard Model. Pioneer in computational approaches to quantum field theory.

faculty.name: Cory Raymond Dean
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Expert in 2D materials and van der Waals heterostructures. Discovered the Hofstadter butterfly in graphene/hBN moiré superlattices and contributed to early observations of correlated phases in twisted bilayer systems.

faculty.name: Tanya Zelevinsky
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Leading experimentalist in ultracold molecular physics and precision spectroscopy. Her group uses laser-cooled and trapped molecules for tests of fundamental physics, including searches for time variation of fundamental constants.

faculty.name: Charles J. Hailey
faculty.title: Pupin Professor of Physics; Co-Director, Columbia Astrophysics Laboratory
faculty.summary: Leading developer of X-ray optics for space missions. Played a key role in the NuSTAR (Nuclear Spectroscopic Telescope Array) mission, the first focusing high-energy X-ray telescope in space. Research spans neutron stars, dark matter searches, and X-ray instrumentation.

faculty.name: Frederik M. Denef
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Prominent string theorist known for foundational work on flux compactifications and the string theory landscape. Contributions to understanding moduli stabilization, supersymmetry breaking, and de Sitter vacua in string theory.

faculty.name: Alberto Nicolis
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Theorist working on formal aspects of quantum field theory and effective field theories. Known for work on the effective field theory of fluids, cosmological perturbations, and the classification of Nambu-Goldstone modes.

faculty.name: Ana Asenjo Garcia
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Rising star in theoretical quantum optics. Studies collective effects in ordered atomic arrays, waveguide quantum electrodynamics, and many-body physics with light-matter interactions. Sloan Fellow and highly cited early-career researcher.

faculty.name: Philip Michael Tuts
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Senior experimentalist on the ATLAS experiment at the LHC. Research includes precision electroweak measurements and tests of the Standard Model. Served in leadership roles in US ATLAS operations.

faculty.name: Yasutomo J. Uemura
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Pioneer in muon spin rotation (muSR) spectroscopy applied to condensed matter systems. Major contributions to understanding unconventional superconductivity, magnetism, and the phase diagram of high-Tc cuprates through muon spin relaxation measurements.

faculty.name: Abhay Narayan Pasupathy
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Expert in scanning tunneling microscopy and spectroscopy of quantum materials. Known for atomic-scale studies of 2D materials, unconventional superconductors, and correlated electron systems.

faculty.name: James W. McIver
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Leading researcher in ultrafast optical control of quantum materials. Uses intense THz pulses and ultrafast spectroscopy to Floquet-engineer topological phases and drive non-equilibrium phase transitions in quantum materials.

faculty.name: J. Colin Hill
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Cosmologist working on CMB analysis, galaxy cluster cosmology, and the Sunyaev-Zeldovich effect. His work constrains dark energy and neutrino physics using combinations of CMB data from ACT, Planck, and large-scale structure surveys. Sloan Fellow.

faculty.name: Kerstin M. Perez
faculty.title: Lavine Family Associate Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Principal investigator of the GAPS (General AntiParticle Spectrometer) balloon experiment, which searches for dark matter signatures through antideuteron detection in cosmic rays. Developing novel particle detection techniques for astroparticle physics.

faculty.name: Gustaaf H. Brooijmans
faculty.title: Professor of Physics; Director of Graduate Studies
faculty.summary: ATLAS experiment physicist searching for new physics beyond the Standard Model at the LHC. Coordinated ATLAS exotic physics searches. Research includes searches for dark matter mediators, heavy resonances, and vector-like quarks. Currently Director of Graduate Studies.

faculty.name: Andrei M. Beloborodov
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Leading theoretical astrophysicist specializing in the extreme physics of neutron stars and magnetars. Key contributions to understanding magnetar flares, pulsar magnetospheres, and radiative processes in strong magnetic fields.

faculty.name: Sebastian Will
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Rising-star experimentalist creating ultracold polar molecules (NaCs) for quantum simulation and computation. His group assembles individual molecules in optical tweezer arrays with single-molecule control.

faculty.name: Federico Capasso
faculty.title: Robert L. Wallace Professor of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Co-inventor of the quantum cascade laser, which revolutionized mid-infrared and terahertz photonics. More recently pioneered metasurface-based flat optics. Recipient of dozens of major awards including the Balzan Prize, Rumford Prize, and the IEEE Edison Medal. Highly Cited Researcher.

faculty.name: David A. Weitz
faculty.title: Mallinckrodt Professor of Physics and of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Co-director of the BASF Advanced Research Initiative at Harvard. Pioneered droplet-based microfluidics, microrheology, and passive particle tracking. His technologies are used globally for single-cell genomics, drug screening, and materials science. Highly Cited Researcher.

faculty.name: Eric Mazur
faculty.title: Balkanski Professor of Physics and of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Pioneer of femtosecond laser materials processing, discovering 'black silicon' and developing applications in photodetectors. Also world-renowned for inventing Peer Instruction, a widely adopted interactive teaching methodology. Member of the National Academy of Education.

faculty.name: Conor J. Walsh
faculty.title: Paul A. Maeder Professor of Engineering and Applied Sciences
faculty.summary: Global leader in soft wearable robotics for human augmentation and rehabilitation. His soft exosuits use textiles and soft actuation to assist mobility-impaired individuals. Work has direct clinical impact for stroke and ALS patients.

faculty.name: Lene Vestergaard Hau
faculty.title: Mallinckrodt Professor of Physics and of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Famous for experimentally stopping light in a Bose-Einstein condensate — a landmark demonstration of quantum optical control. Research has profound implications for quantum information storage and quantum networks. MacArthur Fellow.

faculty.name: Philip Kim
faculty.title: Professor of Physics and of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Leading experimentalist in graphene and 2D materials. His measurements of graphene's electronic properties were foundational to the field. Now working on correlated and topological phases in moiré heterostructures. Member of the National Academy of Sciences. Highly Cited Researcher.

faculty.name: Evelyn Hu
faculty.title: Tarr-Coyne Professor of Applied Physics and of Electrical Engineering
faculty.summary: Pioneer in nanofabrication and quantum devices. Her group develops semiconductor-based quantum photonic devices, including cavity-coupled defect spin qubits in SiC. Major contributions to III-V semiconductor device physics. MacArthur Fellow (1993). Member of the National Academy of Engineering.

faculty.name: Vinothan N. Manoharan
faculty.title: Wagner Family Professor of Chemical Engineering and of Physics
faculty.summary: Area Chair for Applied Physics at SEAS. Studies how colloidal particles self-assemble into complex structures with novel optical properties. His work bridges fundamental soft matter physics with photonic materials and biological assembly.

faculty.name: Zhigang Suo
faculty.title: Allen E. and Marilyn M. Puckett Professor of Mechanics and Materials
faculty.summary: World-leading mechanician studying the deformation, fracture, and adhesion of soft materials. Pioneered highly stretchable and tough hydrogels, ionotronics, and dielectric elastomers. His work enables soft, biocompatible devices and artificial muscles. Highly Cited Researcher.

faculty.name: Donhee Ham
faculty.title: John A. and Elizabeth S. Armstrong Professor of Engineering and Applied Sciences
faculty.summary: Leading researcher bridging integrated circuits and quantum engineering. His group develops cryogenic CMOS control electronics for quantum processors and advanced biosensing platforms. Major innovations in brain-on-chip technologies.

faculty.name: Frans A. Spaepen
faculty.title: John C. and Helen F. Franklin Professor of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Senior figure in materials physics known for foundational work on metallic glasses, colloidal crystals as model systems for studying crystallization, and the thermodynamics of interfaces. Director of the Harvard Materials Research Science and Engineering Center.

faculty.name: Joanna Aizenberg
faculty.title: Amy Smith Berylson Professor of Materials Science; Professor of Chemistry and Chemical Biology
faculty.summary: Pioneer of bio-inspired materials science. Invented SLIPS (Slippery Liquid-Infused Porous Surfaces), a revolutionary omniphobic coating inspired by pitcher plants. Research spans self-healing materials, adaptive optics, and bio-inspired surface engineering. Member of the National Academy of Engineering.

faculty.name: Jennifer Lewis
faculty.title: Hansjorg Wyss Professor of Biologically Inspired Engineering
faculty.summary: World leader in 3D printing of functional, structural, and biological materials. Pioneered multimaterial 3D printing for soft robotics, printed electronics, vascularized tissues, and structural materials. Her innovations are commercialized through multiple startups. Member of the National Academy of Engineering.

faculty.name: Kit Parker
faculty.title: Tarr Family Professor of Bioengineering and Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Army veteran and Harvard professor who builds human heart tissue on chips to study cardiac disease and drug responses. His group bridges fundamental biophysics with tissue engineering, creating models of neuromuscular and cardiac diseases. Multiple startup companies spun out.

faculty.name: Efthimios Kaxiras
faculty.title: John Hasbrouck Van Vleck Professor of Pure and Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Computational physicist developing and applying first-principles electronic structure methods to a broad range of materials problems. Work spans 2D materials, energy conversion, catalysts, and quantum materials. Highly Cited Researcher.

faculty.name: Julia A. Mundy
faculty.title: John L. Loeb Associate Professor of the Natural Sciences and of Engineering and Applied Sciences
faculty.summary: Rising-star experimentalist designing and synthesizing atomically perfect complex oxide heterostructures using molecular beam epitaxy. Her group creates and probes emergent quantum phases at oxide interfaces. Packard Fellow and Sloan Fellow.

faculty.name: David J. Mooney
faculty.title: Robert P. Pinkas Family Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Leader in biomaterials for immunoengineering and tissue regeneration. His group developed implantable biomaterial scaffolds that program immune cells in situ for cancer immunotherapy — a transformational approach now in clinical trials. Member of the National Academy of Engineering.

faculty.name: Katia Bertoldi
faculty.title: William and Ami Kuan Danoff Professor of Applied Mechanics
faculty.summary: Leading researcher in mechanical metamaterials — architected materials with extraordinary mechanical properties derived from their structure rather than composition. Her work on buckling-induced auxetic materials, programmable metamaterials, and soft robotic structures is highly influential.

faculty.name: Boris Kozinsky
faculty.title: Gordon McKay Professor of Materials Science and Mechanical Engineering; Professor of Chemistry and Chemical Biology
faculty.summary: Leader in combining machine learning with first-principles computation for accelerated materials discovery. Research focuses on battery materials, catalysts, and quantum materials. Formerly at Bosch Research, bridging academia and industrial applications.

faculty.name: Michael J. Aziz
faculty.title: Gene and Tracy Sykes Professor of Materials and Energy Technologies
faculty.summary: Pioneer in aqueous organic redox flow batteries for grid-scale energy storage. His group develops long-lifetime organic molecules for cost-effective large-scale batteries. Also known for foundational work on ion-beam processing and laser annealing of semiconductors.

faculty.name: Daniel Needleman
faculty.title: Gordon McKay Professor of Applied Physics and Professor of Molecular and Cellular Biology
faculty.summary: Biophysicist studying the physical principles of self-organization in living cells, particularly the mitotic spindle that segregates chromosomes during cell division. His interdisciplinary work spans physics, biology, and quantitative imaging.

faculty.name: Joost J. Vlassak
faculty.title: Abbott and James Lawrence Professor of Materials Engineering
faculty.summary: Expert in mechanical behavior of thin films and small-scale materials. Research on reliability of microelectronic materials, MEMS devices, and metallization. His combinatorial nanocalorimetry technique enables rapid materials characterization.

faculty.name: Philippe Cluzel
faculty.title: Gordon McKay Professor of Applied Physics and Professor of Molecular and Cellular Biology
faculty.summary: Quantitative biologist studying how cells process information and respond to environmental signals. His group combines microfluidics, time-lapse microscopy, and modeling to understand gene regulation dynamics at the single-cell level.

faculty.name: Zachary Schiffer
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Early-career researcher developing new electrochemical processes for sustainable chemical manufacturing. His research uses fundamental electrochemistry and materials science to enable cleaner production of chemicals and fuels.

faculty.name: Maxim Prigozhin
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Molecular and Cellular Biology and of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Develops new tools combining cryo-EM structural biology with protein engineering to visualize and control cellular signaling. His work enables optical control of specific proteins with potential therapeutic applications.

Fully funded (GSAS PhD guarantee)

faculty.name: Cumrun Vafa
faculty.title: Hollis Professor of Mathematicks and Natural Philosophy; Department Chair
faculty.summary: One of the most influential string theorists in the world. Originator of F-theory and the swampland program. Breakthrough Prize in Fundamental Physics (2017) and Dirac Medal (2008). Member of the National Academy of Sciences.

faculty.name: Andrew Strominger
faculty.title: Gwill E. York Professor of Physics; Director, Center for the Fundamental Laws of Nature
faculty.summary: Towering figure in quantum gravity. Made foundational contributions to black hole physics, including the Strominger-Vafa entropy counting and recent work on black hole soft hair and asymptotic symmetries. Breakthrough Prize (2017).

faculty.name: Mikhail Lukin
faculty.title: Joshua and Beth Friedman University Professor
faculty.sub_field: Atomic, Molecular, and Optical Physics / Quantum Information
faculty.summary: Leading experimentalist in quantum optics and quantum information science. Co-director of the Harvard Quantum Initiative. Pioneered quantum simulation with Rydberg atom arrays and quantum networking with diamond color centers. Member of the National Academy of Sciences. Highly Cited Researcher.

faculty.name: Subir Sachdev
faculty.title: Herchel Smith Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: World-leading condensed matter theorist. Author of the definitive textbook on quantum phase transitions. Developed the SYK model for strange metals and made foundational contributions to understanding high-temperature superconductivity. Dirac Medal (2018). Member of the National Academy of Sciences.

faculty.name: Lisa Randall
faculty.title: Frank B. Baird, Jr. Professor of Science
faculty.sub_field: High Energy Theory / Particle Physics / Cosmology
faculty.summary: One of the most cited particle physicists. Co-originator of the Randall-Sundrum warped extra dimensions model. Author of influential popular science books. Research spans particle physics, cosmology, and dark matter. Member of the National Academy of Sciences.

faculty.name: Philip Kim
faculty.title: Professor of Physics and of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Pioneer in graphene and 2D materials research. Led early experiments measuring graphene's remarkable electronic properties. Now working on moiré quantum matter. Highly Cited Researcher and member of the National Academy of Sciences.

faculty.name: Ashvin Vishwanath
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Prominent condensed matter theorist who predicted Weyl semimetals and contributed foundational ideas to topological insulators and the theory of moiré heterostructures. Highly Cited Researcher. Recipient of the Rahman Prize (2025).

faculty.name: Howard Georgi
faculty.title: Mallinckrodt Professor of Physics
faculty.sub_field: High Energy Theory / Quantum Field Theory
faculty.summary: Pioneer of Grand Unified Theories (GUTs) and co-developer of the Georgi-Glashow SU(5) model. Major contributions to effective field theory and quantum field theory. Longtime Director of Undergraduate Studies and legendary pedagogical influence.

faculty.name: John Doyle
faculty.title: Henry B. Silsbee Professor of Physics
faculty.sub_field: Atomic, Molecular, and Optical Physics / Precision Measurement
faculty.summary: Leading experimentalist in cold molecule physics and precision tests of fundamental symmetries. Co-director of the ACME collaboration measuring the electron electric dipole moment. 2024 Norman F. Ramsey Prize recipient. Member of the National Academy of Sciences.

faculty.name: Markus Greiner
faculty.title: George Vasmer Leverett Professor of Physics
faculty.sub_field: Atomic, Molecular, and Optical Physics / Quantum Simulation
faculty.summary: Pioneer of the quantum gas microscope technique, which enables single-atom, single-site resolved imaging of ultracold atoms in optical lattices. This has become one of the most powerful platforms for experimental quantum simulation. Highly Cited Researcher.

faculty.name: David A. Weitz
faculty.title: Mallinckrodt Professor of Physics and of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: World leader in soft condensed matter physics. Pioneered droplet-based microfluidics and its applications in biology and materials science. His group develops widely used tools for high-throughput single-cell analysis. Highly Cited Researcher.

faculty.name: Daniel Jafferis
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Rising star in theoretical physics. Known for groundbreaking work on the connection between wormholes and quantum teleportation, and for the Gao-Jafferis-Wall traversable wormhole protocol. Frontiers of Science Award recipient.

faculty.name: Matthew Schwartz
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Leading expert in precision QCD calculations for the LHC. Author of the widely adopted graduate textbook 'Quantum Field Theory and the Standard Model.' Research on jet physics, precision collider phenomenology, and effective field theories.

faculty.name: Amir Yacoby
faculty.title: Professor of Physics and of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Leading experimentalist in quantum condensed matter systems. Research on spin qubits in quantum dots, topological insulators, and quantum Hall edge states. Co-developed scanning NV magnetometry techniques for probing magnetic materials.

faculty.name: Norman Yao
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Brilliant theorist working at the intersection of AMO physics, condensed matter, and quantum information. Pioneered Floquet time crystals and made key contributions to many-body localization and quantum sensing protocols. PECASE award (2025). Highly Cited Researcher.

faculty.name: Melissa Franklin
faculty.title: Mallinckrodt Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: First woman tenured in Harvard Physics (1992). Key contributor to the CDF experiment that provided early evidence for the top quark. Currently co-director of the Harvard LPPC and leading ATLAS experiment research. Breakthrough Prize recipient (ATLAS collaboration).

faculty.name: Matthew Reece
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Prominent theorist working on particle physics beyond the Standard Model. Key contributor to axion physics, dark matter model building, and precision tests of fundamental physics. Sloan Fellow.

faculty.name: Kang-Kuen Ni
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry and Chemical Biology; Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Pioneer in assembling individual ultracold molecules from atoms using optical tweezers, enabling full quantum control of molecular quantum states. Opens new frontiers in quantum chemistry and dipolar quantum simulation.

faculty.name: Jenny Hoffman
faculty.title: Clowes Professor of Science
faculty.summary: Expert in scanning tunneling microscopy of quantum materials, including high-temperature superconductors and topological materials. Co-author of the highly influential textbook 'The Physics and Chemistry of Materials.' Also known for completing a cross-country run across the US.

faculty.name: John Huth
faculty.title: Donner Professor of Science
faculty.summary: Longtime ATLAS collaborator studying electroweak physics and Higgs boson production at the LHC. Recipient of the U.S. ATLAS Lifetime Achievement Award. Also known for teaching a popular course on primitive navigation.

faculty.name: Lene Vestergaard Hau
faculty.title: Mallinckrodt Professor of Physics and of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Famous for experimentally slowing light to 17 meters per second and stopping it entirely in a Bose-Einstein condensate. These dramatic demonstrations opened new possibilities for quantum information storage and manipulation.

faculty.name: Xiaowei Zhuang
faculty.title: David B. Arnold Jr. Professor of Science; Professor of Chemistry and Chemical Biology and of Physics
faculty.summary: Inventor of STORM (stochastic optical reconstruction microscopy), a revolutionary super-resolution imaging technique that broke the diffraction limit. Breakthrough Prize in Life Sciences (2019). Member of the National Academy of Sciences. Highly Cited Researcher.

faculty.name: Efthimios Kaxiras
faculty.title: John Hasbrouck Van Vleck Professor of Pure and Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Leading computational physicist developing and applying first-principles methods to study condensed matter systems. Wide-ranging research spanning 2D materials, energy materials, and complex oxides. Highly Cited Researcher.

faculty.name: Carlos Argüelles-Delgado
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Rising star in neutrino physics and multi-messenger astronomy. Group works on IceCube neutrino observatory physics, quantum decoherence searches in neutrino oscillations, and detecting supernova neutrinos with the LHC detectors.

faculty.name: Jordan Cotler
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Sloan Fellow (2024) working at the exciting intersection of quantum information theory, quantum gravity, and high-energy physics. Made key contributions connecting the SYK model, black holes, and quantum chaos.
